retrieved_docs.title: Osteomalacia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Definite osteomalacia is defined as having all of the 5 above findings. Possible osteomalacia is defined as having the first 2 findings and 2 of the 3 remaining findings described above. In another review by Uday and Hogler, new criteria were proposed for the diagnosis of osteomalacia, and include the following: Elevated PTH levels Elevated alkaline phosphatase levels Low urine calcium levels Low calcium intake (usually <300 mg/day) or low calcidiol levels (<30 nmol/L) [22] The use of the above criteria can help with the diagnosis of osteomalacia only in cases where kidney or liver problems are lacking.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopenia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Homocystinuria Hyperparathyroidism Imaging in osteomalacia Mastocytosis Multiple myeloma Paget disease Scurvy Sickle cell anaemia

retrieved_docs.title: Rickets -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Renal insufficiency: Bone disease, also known as renal osteodystrophy, occurs due to many reasons such as the reduced formation of 1,25- dihydroxy vitamin D, metabolic acidosis, retention of phosphate, aluminum toxicity, and secondary hyperparathyroidism.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Kidney Disease-Mineral Bone Disorder (CKD-MBD) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients showing signs and symptoms of bone disease or structural deformities undergo evaluation involving PTH, alkaline phosphatase, calcium, and phosphate levels. Although a history of CKD suggests CKD-MBD, ruling out other bone disorders, such as osteopenia, osteoporosis, vitamin D-resistant rickets, osteopetrosis, and Paget disease of the bone, is crucial to ensure accurate diagnosis and appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteomalacia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the underlying causes of osteomalacia, including vitamin D, calcium, and phosphate deficiencies. Implement appropriate testing, such as serum vitamin D levels, bone mineral density scans, and parathyroid hormone measurements, to help confirm the osteomalacia diagnosis. Apply evidence-based guidelines to select the most suitable treatment modalities, including vitamin D supplementation, dietary adjustments, and underlying disease management. Collaborate with an interprofessional team to coordinate and enhance the delivery of care for patients with osteomalacia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteomalacia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Ergocalciferol is present in plant sources and fortified nutritional alternatives. Cholecalciferol is usually in fish, meat, and eggs. When using vitamin D supplements, accumulating evidence favors the use of cholecalciferol over ergocalciferol because cholecalciferol's side chain has a higher affinity for vitamin D binding protein, thus conferring a longer half-life and a more potent ability to increase vitamin D levels. Since inadequate calcium intake may contribute to the development of osteomalacia, patients should also take at least 1000 mg of calcium per day while being treated for vitamin D deficiency. This dose may need to be increased in patients with malabsorption syndromes, who may also have increased vitamin D dosing requirements. Patients with liver and renal disease may be unable to utilize vitamin D2 or D3 effectively, so calcidiol or calcitriol should be considered. [24] Healing from osteomalacia is achieved when there are increases in urine calcium excretion as well as in BMD. Serum calcium and phosphate may normalize after a few weeks of treatment, but normalization of bone alkaline phosphatase usually lags, and those levels may stay elevated for months. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperchloremic Acidosis(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Conditions that may present with features similar to hyperchloremic acidosis include the following: Non-anion gap metabolic acidosis Vitamin D deficiency Renal tubular acidosis Vitamin D resistance Chronic diarrhea or other extrarenal loss of bicarbonate Monoclonal gammopathy and myeloma Secondary hyperparathyroidism Chronic hypocalcemia Lowe syndrome Sickle cell disease Obstructive uropathy Fabry disease Metachromatic leukodystrophy Methylmalonic acidemia

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperphosphatemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Repeat phosphate estimation should take place to confirm hyperphosphatemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Tubular Acidosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Hypophosphatemia due to decreased proximal phosphate reabsorption and reduced activation of vitamin D also occurs in some patients and may be a major contributor to the development of bone disease. Thus, both phosphate and vitamin D supplementation may be required to normalize the serum phosphate and reverse the metabolic bone disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Secondary Osteoporosis -- Evaluation -- General Screening
retrieved_docs.content: Serum calcium, phosphate, magnesium, and renal function to look for PHPT and renal disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteomalacia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Primary hyperparathyroidism should present with hypophosphatemia, increased bone alkaline phosphatase, and increased zones of uptake. However, it usually presents with hypercalcemia, which is atypical in osteomalacia. In renal osteodystrophy, hyperphosphatemia, rather than hypophosphatemia, is most frequently observed.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypophosphatemia -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment of Hypophosphatemia with Intravenous Replacement Regimens
retrieved_docs.content: With renal dysfunction, the phosphate replacement dose should be reduced by 50%. In patients greater than 130% of their ideal body weight, adjusted body weight should be used to calculate the doses. [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Rickets -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment of rickets due to nutritional deficiency of vitamin D:
retrieved_docs.content: Once the treatment is started, careful monitoring of serum calcium, phosphate, ALP, and 25 hydroxyvitamin D is carried out. [26] Random urine calcium to creatinine ratio should be measured to evaluate any increase in urinary calcium, which is often used to monitor the need to adjust the treatment and also to evaluate for hypercalciuria to prevent nephrocalcinosis. After treatment of the biochemical abnormalities and vitamin D restoration, severe persistent osseous abnormalities may be treated surgically. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Secondary Osteoporosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnoses of secondary osteoporosis include: Osteomalacia Renal osteodystrophy Lymphoma Mastocytosis Sickle cell anemia Multiple myeloma Paget disease Osteonecrosis Infection Homocystinuria Scurvy Homocysteinemia Metastatic bone disease

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1916
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium acetate and calcium phosphate dibasic are both classified as calcium compounds. As members of the broader calcium compounds category, they share the fundamental property of containing calcium ions, yet they are applied differently in medical practice. Calcium acetate is frequently used as a phosphate binder for patients with chronic kidney disease, helping to control serum phosphate levels, while calcium phosphate dibasic serves as a calcium source in nutritional supplements and supports bone health. Recognizing that both agents belong to the calcium compounds group aids clinicians in understanding their chemical characteristics and therapeutic roles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified as a member of the “parathyroid hormones and analogues” group. It is employed therapeutically to correct نقص كالسيوم الدم (blood calcium deficiency or hypocalcemia), reflecting its central role in raising serum calcium levels. Calcitonin is described as having a relationship with parathormon, highlighting the complementary and often opposing actions of these two hormones in the regulation of calcium metabolism. Understanding the classification of parathormon, its use in treating hypocalcemia, and its interaction with calcitonin is essential for managing disorders of calcium balance and for guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2020
retrieved_docs.content: Potassium ion and potassium supplements are directly linked, as potassium supplements are formulations designed to provide potassium ion to the body. By delivering potassium ion, these supplements help correct potassium deficiencies, support normal electrolyte balance, and maintain proper cellular function, making them an important therapeutic option in the management of conditions such as hypokalemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2353
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium protein preparations and calcium lactate are both classified as mineral supplements, representing specific types of calcium‑containing mineral supplement products used to support nutritional needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2023
retrieved_docs.content: Nutritional deficiencies, referred to in Arabic as نقص التغذية, represent a parent category that encompasses a wide range of specific deficiency conditions. This overarching concept includes various forms of inadequate intake or absorption of essential nutrients and serves as the broader classification under which individual deficiency disorders are organized. Recognizing nutritional deficiencies as the parent concept helps clinicians systematically assess and address the diverse manifestations of micronutrient and macronutrient insufficiencies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Kalcitriol and dihydrotachysterol are both classified as members of the “Vitamin D and analogues” group, indicating that they are vitamin D–related compounds that share similar biological activity in regulating calcium and phosphate metabolism. This classification highlights their common therapeutic role as vitamin D analogues used to treat conditions such as hypocalcemia, secondary hyperparathyroidism, and certain bone disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: Oxandrolone, an anabolic androgenic steroid, is contraindicated in patients with hypercalcemia, a disorder marked by elevated serum calcium levels; administering oxandrolone in the setting of hypercalcemia can worsen calcium metabolism disturbances and increase the risk of related complications, so clinicians should avoid prescribing this medication to individuals who have this electrolyte abnormality.
